description_long: This guard stone is a roughly rectangular block of weathered stone, featuring a prominent groove running along one face and a partially hollowed section visible from one side. The surface is uneven, with chipped edges and a mix of gray and white coloration, suggesting exposure to the elements over time. One end appears more eroded, possibly from repeated contact with vehicles or foot traffic, while the other end is more intact. The stone has a coarse, gritty texture, with visible aggregate and occasional patches of discoloration. There are no visible engravings or decorative elements, emphasizing its utilitarian function. The overall form is robust and solid, designed to serve as a protective barrier at the edge of a street or pathway.
description: A weathered, rectangular stone block with a groove and hollowed section, showing chipped edges and a coarse, gray-white surface. Its robust, utilitarian form suggests use as a protective street barrier.
description_view_0: This view highlights the long, flat face with a prominent groove and chipped lower edge, showing the stone's weathered texture.
description_view_1: The hollowed underside and rough, angular edges are visible, revealing the stone's partial cavity and structural wear.
description_view_2: This angle shows the more intact end of the stone, with a triangular recess and a mix of gray and white coloration.
description_view_3: The opposite long face is visible, showing a relatively flat surface with minor chipping and a more uniform texture.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban streets, near the corners of buildings, driveways, or at the entrance to pedestrian zones to protect structures from vehicle impact.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be partially embedded in the ground with the long axis upright and the grooved face exposed to oncoming traffic or vehicles.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible chipping and erosion along the lower edges and corners, likely caused by vehicle contact and weathering. Discoloration and surface smoothing are present on exposed faces, with more pronounced wear on the side facing traffic.
